slides.title: Why researchers reach for the Singulator.
slides.subtitle: Nine peer-reviewed studies where the Singulator beat the alternatives on quality, or did what manual and competing methods could not.
slides.kicker: Selected for differentiation vs competitors + only-possible-with-Singulator · every quote and number verbatim from the paper

slides.title: Sample prep is part of the biology.
slides.body: If dissociation changes which cells survive — or what they express — the sequencing result isn't only measuring the tissue. It's measuring the prep. Manual, hands-dependent isolation can manufacture signal you'll read as biology.
slides.callout: The answer: cold, automated, protocol-controlled isolation removes the uncontrolled variables — so the readout reflects the sample, not the method.
slides.footnote: The fifteen peer-reviewed studies that follow show it two ways: the Singulator beats manual head-to-head, or it does what manual prep cannot.

slides.title: The fifteen studies, at a glance.

groups.label: DIFFERENTIATION VS COMPETITORS — BEATS MANUAL HEAD-TO-HEAD

items.cite: 1.  Kersey et al. · Cell Reports Methods 2026 · mouse brain
items.desc: Beat sucrose-gradient and spin-column prep head-to-head: lowest mito/ribosomal contamination, most consistent QC.

items.cite: 2.  Becker et al. · Nature Genetics 2022 · human colon
items.desc: Singulator scATAC matched manual douncing on paired biopsies — close enough to pool both preps into one atlas.

items.cite: 3.  Perez et al. · Aging 2022 · human muscle
items.desc: Low-artifact prep; 143,051 nuclei surfaced a senescent myonuclei population seen only in aged muscle.

items.cite: 4.  Jessa et al. · Nature Genetics 2022 · pediatric glioma
items.desc: Multiome on 5–20 mg frozen tumor preserved chromatin well enough to fingerprint each glioma's cell of origin.

items.cite: 5.  Dugan et al. · bioRxiv 2024 · human brain
items.desc: 295,299 nuclei from 76 brains on one protocol; the signal survived an explicit prep-quality artifact test.

groups.label: ONLY POSSIBLE WITH THE SINGULATOR — DOES WHAT MANUAL CAN'T

items.cite: 6.  Fisher et al. · Current Protocols 2023 · cervix / uterus
items.desc: Manual dissociation failed; the Singulator 100 delivered Multiome-ready nuclei, adopted as a named HuBMAP method.

items.cite: 7.  Khazaei et al. · Cell 2023 · mouse brain
items.desc: One automated prep fed paired snRNA-seq + snATAC-seq — multiome, the most prep-sensitive assay, off a single isolation.

items.cite: 8.  Haviv et al. · Nature Biotechnology 2024 · FFPE brain
items.desc: A prototype S200+ automated FFPE deparaffinization + nuclei isolation, anchoring Xenium spatial to an snRNA-seq reference.

items.cite: 9.  Pan et al. · JCI Insight 2025 · glioblastoma
items.desc: High-quality snRNA-seq from just 10–20 µg of frozen tumor; 21,959 nuclei resolved MEK-driven MES-like states.

items.cite: 10.  Zhang et al. · Nature Genetics 2026 · pancreas (needle biopsy)
items.desc: 137,491 nuclei from 24 neoplasms — including routine 22G/18G needle-biopsy cores too small to see by eye.

items.cite: 11.  Dermawan et al. · Science Advances 2026 · pediatric sarcoma
items.desc: 177,051 nuclei from 6 patients of an ultrarare sarcoma — only two cases had ever been single-cell profiled.

items.cite: 12.  Blattman et al. · bioRxiv 2026 · FFPE glioblastoma
items.desc: Automated S200+/NIC+ prep recovered 11,192 nuclei from archival FFPE GBM, feeding GIFT genotyping at >99% accuracy.

items.cite: 13.  Abay et al. · Nature Genetics 2025 · FFPE glioblastoma
items.desc: Human FFPE tumor blocks opened to PERFF-seq rare-cell profiling via automated S200+ deparaffinization.

items.cite: 14.  Zhao et al. · Nature Communications 2025 · meningioma
items.desc: Same prep feeds single-cell DNA: paired-meningioma nuclei drove PTA whole-genome sequencing for clonal-evolution CNAs.

items.cite: 15.  Zhang et al. · Nature Communications 2023 · pancreas (automation)
items.desc: Automated under-30-min workflow held up on 38 low-purity PDAC samples — down to one with just 479 captured nuclei.

slides.title: Head-to-head, the Singulator beats manual centrifugation and spin-column on data quality.

citation.ref: Kersey, Acri, Dabin, et al. Comparative analysis of nuclei isolation methods for brain single-nucleus RNA sequencing. Cell Reports Methods, 2026.
citation.author: Jungsu Kim · Indiana University School of Medicine

challenge: Three nuclei-isolation methods compared head-to-head on frozen mouse brain cortex: sucrose-gradient centrifugation, spin-column (10x kit), and the Singulator 100.
Built to settle how isolation protocol choice shapes snRNA-seq data quality and reproducibility.
Brain chosen because microglia readily pick up handling artifacts.

slides.title: Head-to-head, the Singulator beats manual centrifugation and spin-column on data quality.

figure.caption: Figure 3 · QC metrics across the three protocols. Machine-assisted (Singulator, olive) shows the lowest mitochondrial and ribosomal reads (D, E).

quote.text: “The machine-assisted platform provides an automated approach that reduces processing time and minimizes sample-to-sample and person-to-person variability.”

findings: Singulator gave the highest overall data quality: the lowest mitochondrial and ribosomal RNA contamination of the three methods.
It was the most consistent across samples in doublet rate and ambient RNA, where the other two varied widely.
Centrifugation yielded the most nuclei but showed pronounced sample-to-sample heterogeneity.
slides.why: Why it matters:  Direct empirical evidence, in print, that the Singulator out-performs manual centrifugation and spin-column isolation on data quality and cross-sample consistency.

slides.eyebrow: KERSEY ET AL. · FIGURE 3 · QUALITY-CONTROL METRICS
slides.title: Every contaminating read is sequencing budget you can't use.
slides.quote: “Valuable samples and substantial sequencing costs are not wasted due to technical artifacts or contamination by ambient RNAs that necessitate resequencing.”  — Kersey et al.
slides.footnote: Mitochondrial, ribosomal, and ambient reads carry no nuclear signal — every one is sequencing you paid for and discard. The machine-assisted prep minimizes all three, with the least sample-to-sample variation.

slides.eyebrow: KERSEY ET AL. · FIGURE 4 · BIOLOGICAL INTERPRETATION
slides.title: Healthy mice, no disease — yet two methods invent a disease signature.
slides.intro: The mice lacked disease pathology, so a disease-associated-microglia (DAM) signature should be near zero. UCell cell-state scores: panel 4L = homeostatic microglia, 4M = disease-associated microglia.

left.label: MACHINE-ASSISTED · SINGULATOR  —  Fig 4L
left.body: Preserved the strongest homeostatic-microglia markers (C1qa, Hexb, Siglech, Tmem119) and the most accurate transcriptional signature. The DAM signal stays near zero — matching the true biology.

right.label: CENTRIFUGATION & COLUMN  —  Fig 4M
right.body: Both showed an elevated disease-associated-microglia (DAM) signature in disease-free tissue — a technical artifact of the prep, not real biology.

slides.quote: “Because the mice used in this study lacked disease pathology, we expected minimal expression of DAM-related genes. However, both the centrifugation- and column-based methods resulted in elevated DAM signatures, suggesting that technical artifacts, rather than true biological signals, may drive these transcriptional changes.”  — Kersey et al.
slides.footnote: Prep choice flips the conclusion. Only the machine-assisted method matched ground truth (4L); the others manufactured a neuroinflammatory signal (4M) that could misdirect therapeutic development.

slides.eyebrow: DIFFERENTIATION · MATCHES THE MANUAL GOLD STANDARD
slides.title: Singulator nuclei match hand-douncing, on the very same clinical biopsies.

citation.ref: Becker, Nevins, Chen, et al. Single-cell analyses define a continuum of cell state and composition changes in the malignant transformation of polyps to colorectal cancer. Nature Genetics, 2022.
citation.author: William J. Greenleaf · Stanford

challenge: Stanford built a single-cell atlas of the normal-to-polyp-to-cancer continuum from clinical colorectal specimens.
Four samples had nuclei isolated on the S2 Singulator in parallel with manual OmniATAC douncing on the same tissue, for a direct scATAC method comparison.
Compared at the scATAC UMAP, epithelial cell-type composition, and differential-peak levels across the matched samples.

slides.title: Singulator nuclei match hand-douncing, on the very same clinical biopsies.

figure.caption: Figure 1 · Single-cell atlas of colorectal cancer development, built from Singulator- and dounce-prepped nuclei.

stat.body: with hand-douncing: Singulator and dounce scATAC co-embedded by disease state with matching epithelial cell-type fractions on 4 paired colorectal biopsies (Fig. S1J-L).

findings: Singulator and dounce scATAC showed close agreement in UMAP structure and epithelial cell-type fractions on the matched samples.
Both recovered consistent epithelial subtype fractions across sections of the same polyp.
Differential peaks separated sections of the same polyp, not the two prep methods (Fig. S1L).
slides.why: Why it matters:  On precious clinical GI specimens, the Singulator's scATAC nuclei hold up against the manual dounce gold standard, enough that the authors pooled both preps into one atlas.

slides.title: Chosen over conventional prep so the aging signal is biology, not handling.

citation.ref: Perez, Ciotlos, McGirr, et al. Single nuclei profiling identifies cell specific markers of skeletal muscle aging, frailty, and senescence. Aging, 2022.
citation.author: Simon Melov · Buck Institute for Research on Aging

challenge: Bulk RNA-seq, snRNA-seq, and spatial profiling of an irreplaceable cohort of young, aged, and frail human muscle biopsies.
Authors state they chose the Singulator specifically to minimize prep-induced gene-expression changes versus conventional extraction.
Nuclei isolated with S2 cold-buffer protocol; 143,051 nuclei passed QC.

slides.title: Chosen over conventional prep so the aging signal is biology, not handling.

figure.caption: Figure 2 · snRNA-seq resolves 7 muscle cell-type clusters, including the aged-only senescent myonuclei population.

stat.body: nuclei from snap-frozen human muscle; the low-artifact prep surfaced a senescent myonuclei population present only in aged muscle.

findings: A CDKN1A/MYH8-positive senescent myonuclei cluster appeared only in older subjects, validated against drug-induced senescence in culture.
Satellite-cell frequency declined ~20-40% with age; PDK4-positive nuclei rose from 15% to 43%.
Clean prep was the prerequisite: the aging signature had to be real biology, not an extraction stress response.
slides.why: Why it matters:  The Singulator was selected expressly to keep extraction from manufacturing a false signal, the core reproducibility argument, on an aging cohort that cannot be re-collected.

slides.eyebrow: DIFFERENTIATION · PRESERVES CHROMATIN, NOT JUST RNA
slides.title: Nuclei clean enough to fingerprint a tumor's cell of origin.

citation.ref: Jessa et al. K27M in canonical and noncanonical H3 variants occurs in distinct oligodendroglial cell lineages in brain midline gliomas. Nature Genetics, 2022.
citation.author: Claudia L. Kleinman · McGill University

challenge: K27M-mutant pediatric midline gliomas (pons, thalamus, spinal cord) profiled to trace their cell of origin.
5–20 mg of frozen tumor per patient isolated on the Singulator S100 (extended nuclei) for paired snRNA-seq + scATAC-seq.
Lineage read from chromatin accessibility at developmental enhancers — a signal that survives only if the prep preserves chromatin.

slides.title: Nuclei clean enough to fingerprint a tumor's cell of origin.
slides.caption: Figure 5 · chromatin-accessibility lineage fingerprints in K27M glioma (Singulator multiome nuclei).

stat.body: fidelity: Singulator nuclei from 5–20 mg frozen pediatric glioma preserved chromatin accessibility well enough to fingerprint each subtype's embryonic cell of origin (43 patients; 116 tumors integrated).

findings: Paired snRNA-seq + scATAC-seq came off one automated nuclei prep per 5–20 mg tumor.
Quote: “K27M-mutant gliomas faithfully maintain chromatin configuration at developmental genes consistent with anatomically distinct oligodendrocyte-precursor-like cells.”
Chromatin tracks at developmental enhancers mapped each glioma subtype to its distinct OPC-like lineage.
slides.why: Why it matters:  Preserving chromatin — not just RNA — is the harder bar; clearing it on milligram-scale frozen tumor, in Nature Genetics, proves the prep doesn't distort the epigenome.

slides.eyebrow: DIFFERENTIATION · THE SIGNAL SURVIVES AN ARTIFACT TEST
slides.title: 76 brains, one protocol — and the biology survives a QC challenge.

citation.ref: Dugan et al. High-risk neuropsychiatric copy number variants are associated with convergent transcriptomic changes in human brain cells. bioRxiv, 2024.
citation.author: Francis J. McMahon / Anton Schulmann · NIMH

challenge: 76 frozen post-mortem brain samples across 9 high-risk neuropsychiatric CNV loci, matched to controls.
All 76 isolated on the Singulator 100 — one consistent protocol — yielding 295,299 nuclei across 9 cell types.
A convergent mitochondrial (OXPHOS) signal had to be shown to be biology, not a prep batch effect.

slides.title: 76 brains, one protocol — and the biology survives a QC challenge.
slides.caption: Figure 4 · convergent OXPHOS disruption across 9 CNVs (Singulator-isolated nuclei).

stat.body: nuclei from 76 frozen brains on one protocol; a convergent OXPHOS signal held across 9 CNVs even after modeling prep-quality covariates.

findings: One automated protocol across 76 samples made a 9-CNV cross-comparison valid — consistent prep, not batch noise.
Quote: “…the OXPHOS signal was not driven by quality-related variables.”
The authors ran an explicit quality-covariate model; the biological finding held unchanged.
slides.why: Why it matters:  The one study here that explicitly tests whether the result is a prep artifact — and proves it isn't. Consistent automated prep is what made batch effects detectable and excludable.
